title: TmuxInteractiveClaudeBackend wedges forever — Stop sentinel keyed by stale UUID after --resume
description:
Symptom: every flow using PO_BACKEND=tmux (TmuxInteractiveClaudeBackend) hangs at the FIRST step that hits a --resume call (typically baseline/triage iter ≥1, or any later step that resumes a per-role session UUID). Agent finishes work cleanly inside the TUI, writes verdicts and artifacts, then sits at the idle prompt. Orchestrator never advances. Wallclock grows unbounded.

Reproduced 27 Apr 2026, 12:30-12:50 ET: 10+ flows wedged simultaneously (8gc, 5wk, 5wk.1-.6, stf, 7vy, o2r, pw4, 9cn, vtn, h9g, 7hv, polymer-dev-g28zy). Common pattern across all: tmux session alive, agent process responsive (`claude --resume <UUID> --model opus`), verdict files written, sentinel file at `~/.cache/po-stops/<expected-UUID>.stopped` does not exist.

Root cause:

The backend at agent_session.py:673-690 picks new_sid:
- prior + not fork: `new_sid = prior`; cli args `['--resume', prior]`
- fork: `new_sid = uuid4()`; cli args `['--session-id', new_sid, '--resume', prior, '--fork-session']`
- fresh: `new_sid = uuid4()`; cli args `['--session-id', new_sid]`

Then it polls for sentinel `~/.cache/po-stops/<new_sid>.stopped` (line 689).

But Claude Code's `--resume <prior>` DOES NOT preserve the session_id for the resumed turn. It loads the prior conversation state and assigns a brand-new session_id. The Stop hook fires correctly with that NEW id, writing `<new-different-id>.stopped`. Our polled path (`<prior>.stopped`) never appears.

Direct evidence (12:49 ET, while 8gc triager was wedged):
- Live process: `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions --resume b7f267d3-cedb-4b60-9052-fd127d9a1c23 --model opus` (PID 3872999)
- Polled sentinel: `~/.cache/po-stops/b7f267d3-cedb-4b60-9052-fd127d9a1c23.stopped` — MISSING
- Fresh sentinel from same agent (rig=prefect-orchestration, transcript path matches): `~/.cache/po-stops/9e5d555a-6f12-4723-bbc9-08451b28e7b4.stopped` — written at 12:49

Fix options (pick one):

(A) Always pass `--session-id <new>` alongside `--resume <prior>` for non-fork resume. Test whether Claude honors `--session-id` to override the auto-generated post-resume id. If yes, 5-line fix at agent_session.py:674-675. If no, fall through to (B).

(B) Discover the post-resume session_id by watching `~/.claude/projects/<rig-slug>/` for the newest .jsonl mtime after the agent starts, parse first message for session_id, then poll that filename. More moving parts but no Claude-side dependency.

(C) Drop sentinel-polling entirely. Tail the per-role JSONL transcript for the assistant's terminal message (turn-end indicators in the JSONL stream). Most robust but biggest change.

Recommend trying (A) first — if Claude --session-id + --resume together honor the explicit session-id, this is a one-liner.

Also: the stop-hook scope issue. The rig's `.claude/settings.json` installs the Stop hook globally for that cwd, which means EVERY claude session running in that rig (including main interactive Claude Code) fires sentinels into the shared pool. Not a bug per se, just noise — the polled-UUID approach naturally filters it out, but a future improvement could narrow the hook to only fire for sessions matching a per-PO marker.

Workaround for now (pre-fix): `PO_BACKEND=cli po retry <issue> --keep-sessions` — the older ClaudeCliBackend uses subprocess pipes and doesn't depend on Stop-hook sentinels. Lurkable-tmux UX is lost but flows actually progress.

Acceptance:
- Pick fix (A), (B), or (C); document choice in agent_session.py with a why-this comment.
- Re-run a fresh `po run software-dev-full --issue-id <test-bead>` with PO_BACKEND=tmux from cold start. Pipeline must advance past the first --resume call within 60 seconds of the agent finishing turn.
- Add an integration test that mocks the resume path and asserts sentinel discovery (whatever mechanism the fix uses).
- Document the failure mode + workaround at the top of agent_session.py near `_wait_for_stop`.
design: Investigate Claude --session-id+--resume interaction first; if honored, one-line fix. Otherwise watch jsonl mtimes in ~/.claude/projects/<rig>/ to discover post-resume id.
acceptance_criteria: Fix lands; cold po run with PO_BACKEND=tmux completes a multi-iter flow without wedging; integration test for sentinel discovery; doc string updated
close_reason: fixed in f414868 — discover post-resume session_id by matching sentinel transcript content; 8 regression tests in tests/test_agent_session_resume_b9q.py

title: Claude rate-limit not detected as backend failure — only as paste-success marker
description:
`agent_session.py:1100-1114` defines `active_markers` including 'hit your limit'. This is intentional for ONE purpose: detecting that the bracketed-paste landed and Claude is now 'doing something'. The retry loop at line 1115 considers seeing 'hit your limit' equivalent to seeing 'Cogitated' / 'Composing' / 'Thinking' — i.e. a successful submission.

But 'hit your limit' is not a successful submission — it's a terminal failure. After the inner retry-paste loop exits, the code does NOT re-check the pane for rate-limit text. Then it proceeds to _wait_for_stop, which polls forever because the rate-limit terminal turn does not fire a Stop hook (the Stop hook only fires on normal turn completion, see Claude Code docs).

Fix: after the paste-confirmation loop, scan the pane for `'You've hit your limit'`, `'rate limit'`, `'resets <time>'` and raise a clear RateLimitError before entering _wait_for_stop. Include the reset time string in the error so the caller (po retry, beadsd worker) can decide whether to defer + retry or fail.

Bonus: parse the JSONL transcript for the synthetic '<synthetic>' / 'rate_limit' assistant message — that's the deterministic signal Claude itself writes (see /home/ryan-24/.claude/projects/-home-ryan-24-Desktop-Code-personal-storybook/8cc3ba32-2d8d-4082-817d-c5e22a7d0891.jsonl line 60: model='<synthetic>', error='rate_limit', apiErrorStatus=429).
acceptance_criteria: (1) New RateLimitError class in agent_session.py carrying reset_time string. (2) Detected from pane text immediately after submission OR from JSONL synthetic event before _wait_for_stop blocks. (3) prompt() in AgentSession raises it through to caller without nudge retry. (4) Unit test with a fake backend that produces the synthetic rate_limit event in JSONL; assert RateLimitError.
close_reason: Detect Claude rate-limit as terminal backend failure (RateLimitError) via pane scrape + JSONL synthetic-event scan; raised before _wait_for_stop blocks; AgentSession.prompt propagates without nudge retry. 12 new unit tests, 0 regressions. Confidence: MEDIUM-HIGH (live rate-limit smoke not feasible).

title: agent_session._wait_for_stop has no default timeout — rate-limited claude wedges flow forever
description:
`agent_session.py:686` _wait_for_stop signature: `def _wait_for_stop(sentinel, session_name, *, timeout=None, poll=0.4)`. When timeout is None the while loop has no deadline (line 694: `deadline = time.monotonic() + timeout if timeout is not None else None`), so a rate-limited / wedged claude session causes the orchestrator to poll the sentinel file forever.

Observed: storybook sb-atv flow hung 12+ hours after claude hit Anthropic rate limit at 02:47Z. Backend was TmuxInteractiveClaudeBackend; self.timeout_s default is None (`timeout_s: float | None = None` at line 896). Caller (TmuxInteractiveClaudeBackend.run, line 1147) passes `timeout=self.timeout_s` which is None.

Fix: default timeout_s on TmuxInteractiveClaudeBackend (and TmuxClaudeBackend for symmetry) to something like 1800.0 (30 min) — long enough for any legitimate Claude turn including deep cogitation, short enough to not hide a rate-limit. On TimeoutError, raise a RuntimeError naming the issue + including a hint to check for rate-limit / scan ~/.cache/po-stops for the missing sentinel.

Also consider: dropping the entire 'sleep infinity' wrapper for rate-limit cases (separate issue — see -zmb / zombie cleanup).
acceptance_criteria: (1) TmuxInteractiveClaudeBackend default timeout_s is set to a finite value with rationale comment. (2) On _wait_for_stop timeout, the raised exception names the issue, the session_id, and the pane-tail (last ~30 lines) so the operator can see 'You've hit your limit' immediately. (3) Unit test: simulate a Claude that never writes its sentinel; assert RuntimeError within timeout window (use a small timeout for the test).
close_reason: Completed: added DEFAULT_AGENT_TIMEOUT_S=1800.0 finite default on both tmux backends; _wait_for_stop wrapped in run() to raise diagnostic RuntimeError with issue/role/session_id/pane-tail on timeout. 5 new unit tests in tests/test_agent_session_wedge_sav1.py. Confidence: HIGH (verified via unit tests + lint+format clean; pre-existing failures in test_cli_packs/test_deployments/test_mail/test_session_name_derivation are unrelated baseline failures).

title: PO claude rate-limit + transition-hang resilience
description:
Three related PO bugs surfaced during a long storybook epic dispatch (sb-7tk, 18 children, serial chain). Symptoms started after Anthropic rate-limited a long-running claude session at 02:47Z; the flow hung indefinitely instead of failing loudly.

Root-cause walkthrough (artifacts in /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/storybook/.planning/software-dev-full/sb-atv/):
1. Claude session 8cc3ba32-2d8d-4082-817d-c5e22a7d0891 hit '429 You've hit your limit · resets 1:30am ET' during a turn (msg 51 in JSONL).
2. Claude wrote a synthetic 'rate_limit' assistant message and a system 'turn_duration' event, then went idle in ep_poll. The bash wrapper kept the tmux pane alive via 'sleep infinity'.
3. The Stop hook never fires for rate-limited terminal turns, so ~/.cache/po-stops/<sid>.stopped was never written.
4. agent_session._wait_for_stop polls the sentinel file with timeout=None by default — i.e. forever.
5. Operator (me) saw 'Sautéed for 1m 18s' in the pane and assumed the agent was thinking; actually claude was permanently wedged.

The Claude CLI itself (2.1.121) is fine: invoking python3 -m prefect_orchestration.stop_hook manually does write the sentinel correctly. The issue is that PO's contract assumes Claude always fires a Stop hook, and never times out when it doesn't.

Net effect: any rate-limit hit during an epic run wedges the entire chain, and 'po retry' just relaunches into the same wall. Either we fix this in PO or we have to fall back to beadsd for any non-trivial dispatch (beadsd starts a fresh claude per worker so a single rate-limit doesn't poison the chain).
acceptance_criteria: A claude rate-limit during a flow surfaces as a loud RuntimeError naming the issue + cause within ~30 minutes. PO retries are safe to run repeatedly without burning rate-limit budget on stuck sessions. Zombie claude / tmux state is cleaned up on retry/CTRL-C.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: run_tests passes test_cmd to tester prompt (fix KeyError on {{test_cmd}})
description: The tester prompt references {{test_cmd}} but run_tests never provided it, causing KeyError on every flow that reached run_tests. CLAUDE.md mentioned _build_test_cmd as if it existed; it didn't. Add the helper that computes a layer-scoped pytest invocation from rig_path + layer, and pass it via render(). This unblocks: o2r (just hit it), 5wk.6, 5wk.3, 7hv canary, plus all 12 queued flows that were waiting on the dispatcher unstick.
acceptance_criteria: run_tests no longer raises KeyError on {{test_cmd}}; tester prompt receives a layer-correct pytest command; auto-detects tests/unit/, tests/e2e/, tests/playwright/ presence; any flow reaching run_tests can complete the step
close_reason: Closed

title: PO claude subprocesses skip user-level settings (avoid bd-prime contention)
description:
Background: every claude SessionStart runs ~/.claude/settings.json hook 'bd prime', which queries the dolt-server beads backend. When PO fans out 21 parallel claude subprocesses (e.g. epic resume, mass po resume), 21 simultaneous bd prime calls contend on the single dolt-server; some hooks get killed, claude exits 1 mid-startup, the flow fails with 'claude CLI exited 1'. Empirically this still bit a 15s-staggered 21-way wave (16/21 failed). 

Fix: pass '--setting-sources project,local' on PO-spawned claude subprocesses. This loads project + local settings (where PO writes its Stop hook to .claude/settings.local.json) but skips user settings (where the bd-prime hook lives, plus things PO doesn't need: statusLine, mcpServers, alwaysThinkingEnabled, etc.). PO agents use built-in tools (Read/Write/Edit/Bash/Grep); they don't depend on user-level plugins. Stop hook still fires because it's project-level.
acceptance_criteria: claude argv built by _build_claude_argv includes '--setting-sources project,local'; existing Stop-hook flow still works (settings.local.json gets read, sentinel files get written); 21-way po-resume wave no longer fails with 'claude CLI exited 1' on bd-prime contention
notes: Code: prefect_orchestration/agent_session.py::_build_claude_argv at line 66. Both ClaudeCliBackend and TmuxClaudeBackend use this builder so a single change covers both. Verify with 'po resume' on a small batch first.
close_reason: Closed

title: Stop-hook regression: write to settings.local.json (not committed)
description: Implemented in commit 932d96b. _ensure_stop_hook now writes Stop entry to .claude/settings.local.json (gitignored, immune to git restore). Was getting clobbered to HEAD mid-flow. 5 regression tests pin behavior.
close_reason: shipped

title: plan iter 2 for prefect-orchestration-hlk
description:

# Read first

The bead lives in `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration` (`bd` claim/close + `run_dir` here). **Code edits + `git` operations land in `{{pack_path}}`** — often the same path; only different for cross-repo self-dev. Use absolute paths under `{{pack_path}}` when listing affected files.

```bash
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-hlk/CONTEXT.md                 # issue, plan, triage flags, build diff, decision log
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-hlk/baseline.txt               # baseline test state (rig) — NOT in CONTEXT.md
```

Do NOT separately `cat triage.md` / `bd show prefect-orchestration-hlk` — they are already in CONTEXT.md.

# Workflow

## 0. Calibrate effort to scope (read this first)

**Match the plan's depth to the feature's actual complexity.** Read the issue and the affected files first, then size your plan honestly.

- **One-line / one-file change** (registry entry, copy edit, config tweak, missing import, single component prop): write a 3-5 line plan. State the file, the change, the verification. Do NOT write Risks / Test Plan / Research Summary sections — mark them "N/A: scope is one line." A 200-line plan for a 1-line change burns build-iter budget on irrelevant context.
- **Single-purpose module / small feature** (one new component, one new endpoint, one new helper): a paragraph per section is enough. Skip Risks if there genuinely are none beyond "this code didn't exist before."
- **Cross-cutting change** (data model, schema migration, auth path, multi-service contract): full plan with all sections, decision records cross-referenced.

The triager already classified the issue (`/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-hlk/triage.md`) as `trivial | simple | moderate | complex`. **Default your plan size to that tier**:

| Tier | Plan size | Sections |
|---|---|---|
| trivial | ~5 lines | issue + change + check |
| simple | ~30 lines | + files + impl + verification |
| moderate | ~80 lines | + tests + risks |
| complex | full template | all sections, deep |

If your plan is significantly larger than the tier suggests, either (a) the triager mis-classified — surface that — or (b) you're padding. Cut the padding.

**No designed-for-the-future abstractions.** Three similar lines beats a premature helper. A registry of one entry doesn't need a registry — it's a constant. Don't add config knobs, plugin points, or "in case we need it" interfaces unless the issue explicitly requires them.

## 1. Understand the codebase
- Read ALL relevant `CLAUDE.md` files (rig, pack, sub-repos) for architecture + conventions
- **If the repo has `engdocs/` (or equivalent decision-records folder), treat it as ground truth.** Read any `engdocs/architecture/` or `engdocs/design/decisions/` doc that touches the area. If your plan would contradict a decision record, **stop and surface the conflict** — do not silently override.
- Find files relevant to the issue under `{{pack_path}}`
- Identify existing patterns + abstractions you can reuse

## 2. Research external dependencies (if any)
- Look up libraries via `mcp__context7__*` or web search; verify intended usage + current best practices
- For new deps: include a minimal integration test step in your plan before full implementation

## 3. Write the plan

**Match doc depth to change scope.** A one-file utility can have a paragraph per section; an architecture change should have full subsections. Mark genuinely-not-applicable sections "N/A" so it's clear they were considered.

Write to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-hlk/plan.md`. Required sections:

### Issue Summary
- Restate what the issue is asking for (1 paragraph)

### Research Summary

- External library analysis (if applicable)
- Design decisions + trade-offs considered

### Success Criteria

- What does the output/demo look like?

### Files to Modify/Create
- All files (absolute paths under `{{pack_path}}`)
- For new files: justify why
- Skeleton class/function shapes if useful

### Implementation Steps
- High-level steps with **checkpoint** verifications interspersed
- For new libraries: minimal integration test as the first step

### Verification Strategy (mandatory)

For EACH acceptance criterion, give a **concrete check** — specific command, expected output, specific assertion. Not "write a test"; "what command/assertion proves this AC is met."

| Criterion | Verification Method | Concrete Check |
|---|---|---|
| User can create widget | smoke test | `curl -X POST localhost:8000/api/widgets -d '{"name":"x"}' → 201 with id field` |
| Widget appears in list | playwright | navigate to /widgets, assert "x" visible in table |
| Invalid widget rejected | unit test | `test_create_widget_invalid_name()` asserts 422 |

If a criterion can't be auto-verified, say so + propose manual steps.

### Test Plan
- Which test layers apply (unit / playwright / e2e)
- Specific tests to add or modify

### Risks
- Migrations, API contract changes, breaking consumers, cross-boundary consistency
- Anything that needs a rollback plan

{{revision_note}}

# Iterating

If `(no summary captured)` is set, the plan-critic rejected your prior draft. Address every point. Cite the prior critic bead `prefect-orchestration-hlk.plan-critic.iter1` for context. The next critic compares your revised plan to the original critique.

Stage `plan.md` so the critic + builder can read it (no need to commit unless you also produced code skeletons). Reply with one line: `plan complete: <N affected files>`.

title: plan-critic iter 1 for prefect-orchestration-hlk
description:
You are the **plan-critic** for issue `prefect-orchestration-hlk` (plan iter {{plan_iter}}). You audit the plan for completeness, feasibility, and rigor. You do NOT fix the plan — you provide structured feedback for the planner to act on.

You are a cranky senior reviewer with **no investment** in the planner's work. Your job is to find what's missing, vague, or wrong — your critique becomes the next planner's literal input.

# Read first

```bash
bd show prefect-orchestration-hlk                            # original issue
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-hlk/plan.md                          # the plan to review
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-hlk/triage.md                        # routing context
ls /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-hlk                                   # everything else
```

If iter > 1: `{{prior_critique}}` summarises the previous critic's findings; verify the planner addressed each point in the new draft.

# Review rubric

For each section of `plan.md`:

## Completeness
- Does the plan address EVERY acceptance criterion from the issue?
- Are all affected files listed (with absolute paths under `{{pack_path}}`)?
- Is the implementation sequence concrete enough that a builder could follow it without re-designing?

## Verification Strategy (the load-bearing section)
- Is there a concrete verification per AC?
- Are smoke-test commands actually executable (real endpoints, real curl, real assertions)?
- Or vague ("write a test", "check it works") — that's a finding
- For UI features: is there a Playwright assertion?
- For API features: is there a curl + JSON shape check?

## Feasibility
- Does the plan match the existing code architecture? (Cite `engdocs/` if relevant)
- Are the libraries it picks the right tools for the job?
- Are migrations / API breaks called out with a rollback plan?
- Are cross-boundary consistency issues (server↔client, frontend↔backend, schema changes) flagged?

## Risks + omissions
- What could go wrong that the plan doesn't address?
- Hidden coupling? Concurrency? Data integrity? Auth?
- Does the plan over-engineer (premature abstraction, hypothetical future requirements)?
- Does it under-engineer (skipping error paths, ignoring edge cases the issue implies)?

## Decision-record check
- If `engdocs/architecture/` or `engdocs/design/decisions/` has docs covering this area, does the plan respect them?
- Any silent contradiction → **BLOCKING finding**

# Verdict

**Approve when the plan is good enough**, not perfect. Iter cap is enforced; over-rejecting wastes turns.

- **APPROVED** — plan is concrete + addresses all ACs + verification strategy is real
- **REJECTED** — list every concrete finding with file/section refs (your text becomes the next planner's input)

Write your critique to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-hlk/plan-critique-iter-{{plan_iter}}.md`. Be specific: cite plan sections.

# Done — close your bead

Reply with one line: `approved: …` or `rejected: …`.

{{role_step_close_block}}

title: review iter 3 for prefect-orchestration-gub
description:
You are the **build-critic** reviewing build artifacts for issue `prefect-orchestration-gub` (build iter 3). You analyse code for correctness, edge cases, security, and adherence to standards. You do NOT fix code — you provide structured feedback.

You are a cranky senior reviewer with **no investment** in the builder's work. Find what's wrong; your critique becomes the next builder's literal input.

# Read first

```bash
bd show prefect-orchestration-gub                                    # original issue
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/plan.md                                  # what was promised
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/build-iter-3.diff                 # what changed
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/decision-log.md                          # builder's non-obvious choices
[ -f /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/final-tests.txt ] && cat $_             # regression delta
[ -f /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/triage.md ] && cat $_                   # routing context
```

Don't critique a diff in isolation — read the surrounding code in `{{pack_path}}` for context.

# Review rubric

## Correctness
- Does the implementation match the plan?
- Does it fulfil every acceptance criterion (verbatim from the issue)?
- Off-by-ones, null handling, race conditions, type errors?

## Edge cases
- Boundary conditions: empty inputs, null values, large data, unicode
- Error paths: are exceptions caught at the right level? Are error messages useful?
- Concurrency: shared state, file locks, atomic ops where required

## Security (BLOCKING when present)
- Input validation, sanitisation
- SQL injection, XSS, command injection, path traversal
- Secrets / credentials in code, logs, or git history
- Authentication / authorisation bypass

## Anti-mock checklist (BLOCKING — any violation must be fixed before approval)

LLM builders love to mock, stub, and fake things. This is the #1 source of "tests pass but feature doesn't work."

**Production code (NEVER acceptable):**
- Hardcoded sample/placeholder data (`# TODO: replace with real data`, lorem ipsum, fake user IDs, `example.com` URLs in non-test code)
- Stubbed functions returning fake results (`return {"status": "ok"}`, `return []`, `return True` without doing the work)
- Commented-out real implementation with fake fallback
- Feature flags defaulting to mock mode (`USE_REAL_API = False`)
- In-memory stores replacing real persistence (unless plan explicitly calls for it)
- Fake auth (middleware that always returns True / always assigns admin)
- Print/log instead of actual side effects (`print(f"Would send email to {user}")`)

**Test code (acceptable in unit tests, NOT in integration/e2e):**
- Integration tests that mock the thing they're integrating with
- Tests that only verify mocks were called (not what happens when the real thing responds)
- Fixtures returning hardcoded dicts instead of real DB/file state
- Snapshot tests of mock responses (circular)

**Data quality:**
- Seed/test data missing required fields with realistic values (`name="test"`, `email="a@b.com"`, `price=0`)
- Happy path implemented but error responses are `pass` / `return None`
- Placeholder UI text in shipped components

When you find a violation, flag it as **BLOCKING** with file:line + expected behaviour:

```
BLOCKING: Mock/placeholder detected in production code
File: app/services/widget_service.py:45
Issue: Function returns hardcoded list instead of querying database
Expected: Query the widgets table and return real results
```

## Style / conventions
- Matches existing patterns in this sub-repo?
- Imports at the top (not in functions)
- Type hints, dataclasses, no f-string in `logger.*`
- Comments explain WHY, not WHAT (delete comments that just narrate the code)

## Risk
- Could this break anything else? Cross-boundary consistency?
- API contract changes that break consumers?
- Migrations that need a rollback plan?

## Decision log audit
- Are the builder's non-obvious choices justified? Reference plan sections / CLAUDE.md / engdocs?
- Decisions without rationale are findings — mark them.

# Verdict

**Approve when work is good enough**, not perfect. Iter cap is enforced by the orchestrator.

- **APPROVED** — meets ACs, no blocking findings, no anti-mock violations, no regressions
- **REJECTED** — list every concrete finding (file:line + expected fix); your text becomes the next builder's input

Write your critique to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/critique-iter-3.md`.

# Iterating

# Done — close your bead

Reply with one line: `approved: …` or `rejected: …`.

title: test-unit iter 3 for prefect-orchestration-gub
description:
You are the **tester** running `unit` tests for issue `prefect-orchestration-gub` (iter 3). You write missing tests for the new code AND run the existing suite, scoped to the diff. You do NOT alter production code; only test code under `tests/`.

# Layer definitions (non-overlapping)

- **unit** — individual functions / classes in isolation. Mocking external services (HTTP, DB, subprocess) is fine. No real network, no real subprocesses, no Prefect server. Lives at top of `tests/` (or `tests/unit/` when present).
- **e2e** — integration across **real** dependencies. No mocking of the things under integration. Slower (seconds to minutes per test). Lives at `tests/e2e/`.
- **playwright** — browser-driven UI tests. Skip when there's no UI. Lives at `tests/playwright/`.

If you're writing a test that mocks subprocess calls, it's a unit test. If it spawns the real binary or hits a real Prefect server, it's e2e. Don't put both kinds in the same file.

# Read first

The orchestrator wrote `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/CONTEXT.md` containing the plan, triage flags,

already in the bundle. Re-running them wastes 5–8s per round-trip.


# Workflow

## 1. Identify gaps

For each AC the build claims to satisfy, check whether a `unit` test covers it. If not, write one — alongside the existing patterns (don't introduce a new test framework).

Test-design rules:
- Test the user-facing contract, not the implementation. If the code refactors but behaviour stays the same, the test should still pass.
- One assertion per concept (multiple `assert` lines are fine if they're checking different facets of one behaviour).
- Real fixtures over mocked dicts (especially in e2e — see anti-mock list below).
- Cover error paths, not just happy paths.

## 2. Diff-mapped scope (avoid running the entire suite)

The orchestrator computes test scope from the diff. If `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/tests-changed.txt` exists, use it:

```bash

[ -z "$SCOPE" ] && SCOPE="tests/unit/"     # fall back to full layer
```

Otherwise default to `tests/unit/`.

## 3. Run the unit tests

```bash
cd /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration
uv run python -m pytest $SCOPE --tb=short 2>&1 | tee /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/unit-iter-3.log
```

(Adapt the runner if the project isn't Python: `bun test`, `make test`, etc.)

## 4. Triage failures

For each failing test:
- **Pre-existing failure** (also fails on `git stash` of build) → not yours; note in log + skip
- **Regression** caused by the build → REJECT (don't fix; that's the builder's job)
- **Failing test you just wrote against the build** → fix the test if it's wrong; otherwise REJECT

# Anti-mock for tests

- Integration / e2e tests that mock the thing they're integrating with → not real tests
- Tests that only verify mocks were called → prove nothing about real behaviour
- Snapshot tests of mock returns → circular
- Fixtures returning hardcoded dicts where real DB/file state belongs

If you find existing tests like this on the diff, flag them in the log (don't fix them as part of this iter — the builder owns that fix).

# Verdict

- **PASSED** — every test in scope passed; no regressions vs baseline; gaps filled with new tests
- **FAILED** — regression(s) detected, OR test gaps you can't fill without altering production code

Append the verdict line to the log, then bead notes:

```bash
bd update prefect-orchestration-gub.test-unit.iter3 --append-notes "<one-line summary>"
```

# Done — close your bead

Reply with one line: `passed: <N tests>` or `failed: <one-line summary>`.

# File reservations (concurrent-worker hygiene)

The pack uses `mcp-agent-mail` reservations to prevent same-file collisions across PO workers running concurrent issues:

1. At role start, ensure the project + register an agent name `"prefect-orchestration-gub-builder"`:

- **Don't add error handling, fallbacks, or validation for scenarios that can't happen.** Trust internal callers; only validate at system boundaries.
- **Don't write multi-paragraph docstrings** explaining what well-named code already shows. One short line max for non-obvious WHY.

If the plan itself looks padded for the actual scope, push back: write a one-paragraph note in `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/decision-log.md` explaining what you trimmed and why, and implement the minimal correct version. # Review rubric

## Correctness
- Does the implementation match the plan?
- Does it fulfil every acceptance criterion (verbatim from the issue)?
- Off-by-ones, null handling, race conditions, type errors?

## Edge cases
- Boundary conditions: empty inputs, null values, large data, unicode
- Error paths: are exceptions caught at the right level? Are error messages useful?
- Concurrency: shared state, file locks, atomic ops where required

## Security (BLOCKING when present)
- Input validation, sanitisation
- SQL injection, XSS, command injection, path traversal
- Secrets / credentials in code, logs, or git history
- Authentication / authorisation bypass

## Anti-mock checklist (BLOCKING — any violation must be fixed before approval)

LLM builders love to mock, stub, and fake things. This is the #1 source of "tests pass but feature doesn't work."

**Production code (NEVER acceptable):**
- Hardcoded sample/placeholder data (`# TODO: replace with real data`, lorem ipsum, fake user IDs, `example.com` URLs in non-test code)
- Stubbed functions returning fake results (`return {"status": "ok"}`, `return []`, `return True` without doing the work)
- Commented-out real implementation with fake fallback
- Feature flags defaulting to mock mode (`USE_REAL_API = False`)
- In-memory stores replacing real persistence (unless plan explicitly calls for it)
- Fake auth (middleware that always returns True / always assigns admin)
- Print/log instead of actual side effects (`print(f"Would send email to {user}")`)

**Test code (acceptable in unit tests, NOT in integration/e2e):**
- Integration tests that mock the thing they're integrating with
- Tests that only verify mocks were called (not what happens when the real thing responds)
- Fixtures returning hardcoded dicts instead of real DB/file state
- Snapshot tests of mock responses (circular)

**Data quality:**
- Seed/test data missing required fields with realistic values (`name="test"`, `email="a@b.com"`, `price=0`)
- Happy path implemented but error responses are `pass` / `return None`
- Placeholder UI text in shipped components

When you find a violation, flag it as **BLOCKING** with file:line + expected behaviour:

```

Issue: Function returns hardcoded list instead of querying database
Expected: Query the widgets table and return real results
```

## Style / conventions
- Matches existing patterns in this sub-repo?
- Imports at the top (not in functions)
- Type hints, dataclasses, no f-string in `logger.*`
- Comments explain WHY, not WHAT (delete comments that just narrate the code)

## Risk
- Could this break anything else? Cross-boundary consistency?
- API contract changes that break consumers?
- Migrations that need a rollback plan?

## Decision log audit
- Are the builder's non-obvious choices justified? Reference plan sections / CLAUDE.md / engdocs?
- Decisions without rationale are findings — mark them.

# Verdict

**Approve when work is good enough**, not perfect. Iter cap is enforced by the orchestrator.

- **APPROVED** — meets ACs, no blocking findings, no anti-mock violations, no regressions
- **REJECTED** — list every concrete finding (file:line + expected fix); your text becomes the next builder's input

Write your critique to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/critique-iter-2.md`.

title: test-unit iter 2 for prefect-orchestration-gub

# Layer definitions (non-overlapping)

- **unit** — individual functions / classes in isolation. Mocking external services (HTTP, DB, subprocess) is fine. No real network, no real subprocesses, no Prefect server. Lives at top of `tests/` (or `tests/unit/` when present).
- **e2e** — integration across **real** dependencies. No mocking of the things under integration. Slower (seconds to minutes per test). Lives at `tests/e2e/`.
- **playwright** — browser-driven UI tests. Skip when there's no UI. Lives at `tests/playwright/`.

If you're writing a test that mocks subprocess calls, it's a unit test. If it spawns the real binary or hits a real Prefect server, it's e2e. Don't put both kinds in the same file.

# Workflow

## 1. Identify gaps

For each AC the build claims to satisfy, check whether a `unit` test covers it. If not, write one — alongside the existing patterns (don't introduce a new test framework).

Test-design rules:
- Test the user-facing contract, not the implementation. If the code refactors but behaviour stays the same, the test should still pass.
- One assertion per concept (multiple `assert` lines are fine if they're checking different facets of one behaviour).
- Real fixtures over mocked dicts (especially in e2e — see anti-mock list below).
- Cover error paths, not just happy paths.

## 2. Diff-mapped scope (avoid running the entire suite)

The orchestrator computes test scope from the diff. If `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/tests-changed.txt` exists, use it:

## 3. Run the unit tests

## 4. Triage failures

For each failing test:

- **Regression** caused by the build → REJECT (don't fix; that's the builder's job)

# Anti-mock for tests

- Integration / e2e tests that mock the thing they're integrating with → not real tests
- Tests that only verify mocks were called → prove nothing about real behaviour
- Snapshot tests of mock returns → circular
- Fixtures returning hardcoded dicts where real DB/file state belongs

If you find existing tests like this on the diff, flag them in the log (don't fix them as part of this iter — the builder owns that fix).

# Verdict

- **PASSED** — every test in scope passed; no regressions vs baseline; gaps filled with new tests

title: triage iter 1 for prefect-orchestration-hlk
description:
You are the **triager** for issue `prefect-orchestration-hlk` (rig `{{rig}}` at `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration`, code at `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration`).

```bash
[ -d /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration ] && ls /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration   # where code edits will land
```

# Job

1. Classify routing flags (downstream roles read these to decide what to skip / run)
2. Pick a **complexity tier** (controls how much pipeline runs)
3. (Optional) for `trivial` issues: do the work yourself + close + return

# Routing flags

- `has_ui` — does this change UI? (gates demo-video step)
- `has_backend` — does this change backend?
- `needs_migration` — schema/DB change?
- `is_docs_only` — docs/comments only, no code? (short-circuits to docs+learn only)

# Complexity tiers (PICK ONE)

| Tier | What runs | Wall-clock | When to pick |
|---|---|---|---|
| **`trivial`** | triager only — you do the work + close, no plan/build/review/test | ~2 min | Typo fix, rename a variable, bump a constant, fix a comment, single-line config tweak, stale-doc cleanup. The change is so obvious + isolated that planning would be ceremony. |
| **`simple`** | triage → plan → build → lint → close (no critics, no tests, no review) | ~10 min | Small bug fix, tiny refactor, single-file feature where the build will be obviously right. Plan is short; lint catches mechanics; no need for a critic to second-guess. |
| **`moderate`** | + plan-critic + build-critic + unit tests + docs + learn (no e2e/playwright, no regression-gate, no live verifier, no ralph, no full-test-gate) | ~20-30 min | Small-to-medium feature, multi-file change with tests, anything where a code reviewer would want to look at the diff. Critics catch real mistakes; unit tests cover the new code. |
| **`complex`** | + e2e/playwright (if has_ui) + regression-gate + deploy-smoke + verifier + demo-video. Ralph + full-test-gate are OPT-IN flags, off by default. | ~30-40 min | Production-impacting change: API contract change, schema/DB migration, security-sensitive code path, breaking change to a published interface, multi-package refactor crossing repo boundaries. |

**Bias toward the lighter tier when uncertain.** The pipeline is expensive — `complex` costs ~30-40 min of API time vs `moderate`'s ~20-25 min vs `simple`'s ~10 min. Picking `trivial` for something that wasn't is the only failure mode that ships unreviewed code; for everything else, `simple` or `moderate` still go through plan + build + lint, and `moderate` adds critics + tests. **You almost never need `complex`** — reserve it for changes whose blast radius is "this could break production for users." Documentation, internal tooling, refactors-with-tests, new features behind a feature flag — all `moderate` or below.

# Trivial path (do-the-work-yourself)

If you picked `trivial`:

1. Make the edit yourself. Use Edit tool to change the file(s).
2. `cd /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration` then `git add <files>` + `git commit -m "[prefect-orchestration-hlk] <one-line>"`.
3. Verify the change makes sense (re-read the diff with `git -C /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration diff HEAD~1`).
4. Skip the regular triage.json verdict file — just write `triage.json` with `complexity=trivial` flag set so the flow body knows you handled it.

If at any point you realize this isn't actually trivial (the change is bigger than expected, or you can't make the edit cleanly), STOP, set `complexity=simple` or `moderate`, and let the regular pipeline handle it.

# Write the triage summary

description:
You are the **build-critic** reviewing build artifacts for issue `prefect-orchestration-gub` (build iter 1). You analyse code for correctness, edge cases, security, and adherence to standards. You do NOT fix code — you provide structured feedback.

You are a cranky senior reviewer with **no investment** in the builder's work. Find what's wrong; your critique becomes the next builder's literal input.

# Read first

```bash
bd show prefect-orchestration-gub                                    # original issue
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/plan.md                                  # what was promised
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/build-iter-1.diff                 # what changed
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/decision-log.md                          # builder's non-obvious choices
[ -f /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/final-tests.txt ] && cat $_             # regression delta
[ -f /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/triage.md ] && cat $_                   # routing context
```

Don't critique a diff in isolation — read the surrounding code in `{{pack_path}}` for context.

# Review rubric

## Correctness
- Does the implementation match the plan?
- Does it fulfil every acceptance criterion (verbatim from the issue)?
- Off-by-ones, null handling, race conditions, type errors?

## Edge cases
- Boundary conditions: empty inputs, null values, large data, unicode
- Error paths: are exceptions caught at the right level? Are error messages useful?
- Concurrency: shared state, file locks, atomic ops where required

## Security (BLOCKING when present)
- Input validation, sanitisation
- SQL injection, XSS, command injection, path traversal
- Secrets / credentials in code, logs, or git history
- Authentication / authorisation bypass

## Anti-mock checklist (BLOCKING — any violation must be fixed before approval)

LLM builders love to mock, stub, and fake things. This is the #1 source of "tests pass but feature doesn't work."

**Production code (NEVER acceptable):**
- Hardcoded sample/placeholder data (`# TODO: replace with real data`, lorem ipsum, fake user IDs, `example.com` URLs in non-test code)
- Stubbed functions returning fake results (`return {"status": "ok"}`, `return []`, `return True` without doing the work)
- Commented-out real implementation with fake fallback
- Feature flags defaulting to mock mode (`USE_REAL_API = False`)
- In-memory stores replacing real persistence (unless plan explicitly calls for it)
- Fake auth (middleware that always returns True / always assigns admin)
- Print/log instead of actual side effects (`print(f"Would send email to {user}")`)

**Test code (acceptable in unit tests, NOT in integration/e2e):**
- Integration tests that mock the thing they're integrating with
- Tests that only verify mocks were called (not what happens when the real thing responds)
- Fixtures returning hardcoded dicts instead of real DB/file state
- Snapshot tests of mock responses (circular)

**Data quality:**
- Seed/test data missing required fields with realistic values (`name="test"`, `email="a@b.com"`, `price=0`)
- Happy path implemented but error responses are `pass` / `return None`
- Placeholder UI text in shipped components

When you find a violation, flag it as **BLOCKING** with file:line + expected behaviour:

```
BLOCKING: Mock/placeholder detected in production code
File: app/services/widget_service.py:45
Issue: Function returns hardcoded list instead of querying database
Expected: Query the widgets table and return real results
```

## Style / conventions
- Matches existing patterns in this sub-repo?
- Imports at the top (not in functions)
- Type hints, dataclasses, no f-string in `logger.*`
- Comments explain WHY, not WHAT (delete comments that just narrate the code)

## Risk
- Could this break anything else? Cross-boundary consistency?
- API contract changes that break consumers?
- Migrations that need a rollback plan?

## Decision log audit
- Are the builder's non-obvious choices justified? Reference plan sections / CLAUDE.md / engdocs?
- Decisions without rationale are findings — mark them.

# Verdict

**Approve when work is good enough**, not perfect. Iter cap is enforced by the orchestrator.

- **APPROVED** — meets ACs, no blocking findings, no anti-mock violations, no regressions
- **REJECTED** — list every concrete finding (file:line + expected fix); your text becomes the next builder's input

Write your critique to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/critique-iter-1.md`.

# Iterating

If `{{prior_critique}}` is set, the prior critic also rejected this build. Verify the new diff actually addresses each point in `{{prior_critic_bead}}`'s critique. Findings the builder ignored = repeat-blocking finding.

# Done — close your bead

Reply with one line: `approved: …` or `rejected: …`.

Do NOT separately `cat triage.md` / `bd show prefect-orchestration-gub` — they are already in CONTEXT.md.

# Workflow

## 0. Calibrate effort to scope (read this first)

**Match the plan's depth to the feature's actual complexity.** Read the issue and the affected files first, then size your plan honestly.

- **One-line / one-file change** (registry entry, copy edit, config tweak, missing import, single component prop): write a 3-5 line plan. State the file, the change, the verification. Do NOT write Risks / Test Plan / Research Summary sections — mark them "N/A: scope is one line." A 200-line plan for a 1-line change burns build-iter budget on irrelevant context.
- **Single-purpose module / small feature** (one new component, one new endpoint, one new helper): a paragraph per section is enough. Skip Risks if there genuinely are none beyond "this code didn't exist before."
- **Cross-cutting change** (data model, schema migration, auth path, multi-service contract): full plan with all sections, decision records cross-referenced.

The triager already classified the issue (`/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/triage.md`) as `trivial | simple | moderate | complex`. **Default your plan size to that tier**:

| Tier | Plan size | Sections |
|---|---|---|
| trivial | ~5 lines | issue + change + check |
| simple | ~30 lines | + files + impl + verification |
| moderate | ~80 lines | + tests + risks |
| complex | full template | all sections, deep |

If your plan is significantly larger than the tier suggests, either (a) the triager mis-classified — surface that — or (b) you're padding. Cut the padding.

**No designed-for-the-future abstractions.** Three similar lines beats a premature helper. A registry of one entry doesn't need a registry — it's a constant. Don't add config knobs, plugin points, or "in case we need it" interfaces unless the issue explicitly requires them.

## 1. Understand the codebase
- Read ALL relevant `CLAUDE.md` files (rig, pack, sub-repos) for architecture + conventions

If your plan would contradict a decision record, **stop and surface the conflict** — do not silently override.
- Find files relevant to the issue under `{{pack_path}}`
- Identify existing patterns + abstractions you can reuse

## 2. Research external dependencies (if any)
- Look up libraries via `mcp__context7__*` or web search; verify intended usage + current best practices
- For new deps: include a minimal integration test step in your plan before full implementation

## 3. Write the plan

**Match doc depth to change scope.** A one-file utility can have a paragraph per section; an architecture change should have full subsections. Mark genuinely-not-applicable sections "N/A" so it's clear they were considered.

Write to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gub/plan.md`. Required sections:

### Issue Summary
- Restate what the issue is asking for (1 paragraph)

### Research Summary

- External library analysis (if applicable)

### Success Criteria

### Files to Modify/Create

- For new files: justify why
- Skeleton class/function shapes if useful

### Implementation Steps
- High-level steps with **checkpoint** verifications interspersed
- For new libraries: minimal integration test as the first step

### Verification Strategy (mandatory)

For EACH acceptance criterion, give a **concrete check** — specific command, expected output, specific assertion. Not "write a test"; "what command/assertion proves this AC is met."

| Criterion | Verification Method | Concrete Check |
|---|---|---|
| User can create widget | smoke test | `curl -X POST localhost:8000/api/widgets -d '{"name":"x"}' → 201 with id field` |
| Widget appears in list | playwright | navigate to /widgets, assert "x" visible in table |
| Invalid widget rejected | unit test | `test_create_widget_invalid_name()` asserts 422 |

If a criterion can't be auto-verified, say so + propose manual steps.

### Test Plan

### Risks
- Migrations, API contract changes, breaking consumers, cross-boundary consistency
- Anything that needs a rollback plan

title: agent_step hangs silently when Claude SDK returns 429-shaped success result
description:
Repro: PO runs (nanocorps-0k8 first dispatch, prefect-orchestration-gub multiple times today) hang at a step (e.g. baseline-iter-1) for 15-50+ minutes with the Claude subprocess alive but doing no work (~0 CPU). Run-dir shows the previous artifact (baseline.txt) written, but no further activity (CONTEXT.md / role-session.json static).

## Diagnosis

agent_step.py:666-685 retries on RateLimitError by rotating OAuth pool slots. That loop works when the SDK actually raises RateLimitError. But the Claude SDK can also return a success-shaped result envelope that is internally a 429:

  {"type":"result","subtype":"success","is_error":true,"api_error_status":429,"result":"You've hit your limit · resets 12:50pm (America/New_York)","terminal_reason":"completed",...}

(observed verbatim in nanocorps-0k8's first PO log). When this shape comes back, the wrapper either:
- Doesn't classify it as RateLimitError and proceeds with empty content, then the next agent_step blocks forever waiting for downstream input that never arrives, OR
- Detects rate-limit and raises, but the rotated OAuth slot also returns the same shape, and we never make progress.

In either case agent_step has no:
- wall-clock timeout on sess.prompt() / the underlying subprocess
- heartbeat / role-session-file-mtime watchdog
- detection of repeated 429-shaped success envelopes (>= K consecutive)

So the run sits in 'Running' state in Prefect indefinitely. Operator has to ps + kill manually. Today this hit at least: nanocorps-0k8 (first attempt), prefect-orchestration-gub (twice), and ~30 zombie subprocesses from this morning's parallel nanocorps-lks children (2d1/jgg/fb8/tbw) all stuck the same way.

## Proposed fixes (any one would help; all three are the durable answer)

1. **Wall-clock timeout on agent_step's subprocess wait.** Configurable per-step (e.g. baseline=10min, build=30min, review=10min). On expiry: kill subprocess + raise StepTimeoutError so Prefect marks the run Failed and the operator can po retry.
2. **Heartbeat watchdog** on the run-dir: monitor role-session.json + CONTEXT.md mtime. If no write in M minutes during a long-running step, treat as wedged + kill.
3. **Detect 429-success-envelope** explicitly in the SDK response parser. If api_error_status==429 in a success-shaped result, raise RateLimitError so the existing retry loop fires (or fails out cleanly when failovers exhausted).

## Acceptance

- agent_step has a configurable per-step timeout; default values shipped for triage / baseline / plan / build / lint / test-unit / review / docs / learn.
- A simulated 429-success-envelope (test fixture) raises RateLimitError instead of returning empty.
- Documentation in agent_step.py docstring describes the failure mode + the timeout behavior.
- A regression test that simulates a wedged subprocess (no stdout, no exit) and asserts agent_step times out within the configured budget rather than hanging.

## Why P2 not P1

P1 if the user is dispatching new epics today; P2 if the workaround (manual ps + kill) is acceptable for the next few days. Adjustable on landing.
acceptance_criteria: agent_step.py has wall-clock per-step timeout; 429-success-envelope shape raises RateLimitError; wedged-subprocess regression test in tests/ asserts timeout fires; no longer manually killing zombie claude processes after rate-limit incidents.

title: learn iter 1 for prefect-orchestration-tvo
description:
You are the **learn agent** processing lessons-learned from this issue's run, surfacing patterns + gotchas worth promoting to CLAUDE.md.

You only update CLAUDE.md when there's a meaningful, durable insight. Don't pad. Don't echo what's already there.

# Read first

```bash
bd show prefect-orchestration-tvo                                          # what shipped
ls /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo                                                  # all run artifacts
[ -f /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/lessons-learned.md ] && cat $_                 # appended-to throughout the flow
[ -f /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/decision-log.md ] && cat $_                    # builder's non-obvious choices
ls /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/critique-iter-*.md 2>/dev/null && cat $_         # what the critic caught
ls /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/verification-report-iter-*.md 2>/dev/null        # what the verifier flagged
```

# What to look for

Durable, project-specific learnings. Skip the obvious + the LLM-generic.

| Promote | Skip |
|---|---|
| "X library has a footgun: Y" | "use functions to organise code" |
| "Our schema convention requires X" | "always write tests" |
| "This sub-repo's lint rule prefers Y" | generic best practices |
| "Pattern X recurred 3 times this run; abstract" | one-off implementation detail |
| Surprising debugging path the build took | every test that passed |

# Where to update

| Insight scope | Target |
|---|---|
| Affects ALL projects | `~/.claude/CLAUDE.md` (use sparingly) |
| Affects this rig only | `<rig_path>/CLAUDE.md` |
| Affects this sub-repo only | `<sub_repo>/CLAUDE.md` (e.g. `{{pack_path}}/CLAUDE.md`) |
| Affects this directory only | `<dir>/CLAUDE.md` |

Place insights at the most-specific level that's still correct. Don't promote to a parent unless the pattern actually generalises.

# Update protocol

1. Read the target CLAUDE.md before editing
2. Find the most relevant existing section (don't create a new top-level if a 2-line addition fits an existing one)
3. Add the insight as a single bullet OR a 2-3 line note
4. Avoid duplicating across CLAUDE.md files — pick ONE location
5. After editing, `cd` into the right git repo and commit:

```bash
cd <target-claude-md-repo>
git add CLAUDE.md
git commit -m "[prefect-orchestration-tvo] CLAUDE.md: <one-line insight>"
```

# When there are no meaningful learnings

That's a valid outcome. Append a single line to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/lessons-learned.md`:

```
- No meaningful CLAUDE.md updates from prefect-orchestration-tvo — implementation matched plan + existing conventions, no surprises.
```

Don't invent insights to fill space.

# Done — close your bead

Reply with one line: `learn complete: <N CLAUDE.md files updated>` or `learn complete: no meaningful updates`.

{{role_step_close_block}}

title: docs iter 1 for prefect-orchestration-tvo
description:

Update sub-repo `CLAUDE.md` / `docs/` / `README.md` based on what shipped. Most small changes need no doc updates — if so, reply `no docs needed` and close out.

description:

# Layer definitions (non-overlapping)

- **unit** — individual functions / classes in isolation. Mocking external services (HTTP, DB, subprocess) is fine. No real network, no real subprocesses, no Prefect server. Lives at top of `tests/` (or `tests/unit/` when present).
- **e2e** — integration across **real** dependencies. No mocking of the things under integration. Slower (seconds to minutes per test). Lives at `tests/e2e/`.
- **playwright** — browser-driven UI tests. Skip when there's no UI. Lives at `tests/playwright/`.

If you're writing a test that mocks subprocess calls, it's a unit test. If it spawns the real binary or hits a real Prefect server, it's e2e. Don't put both kinds in the same file.

# Workflow

## 1. Identify gaps

For each AC the build claims to satisfy, check whether a `unit` test covers it. If not, write one — alongside the existing patterns (don't introduce a new test framework).

Test-design rules:
- Test the user-facing contract, not the implementation. If the code refactors but behaviour stays the same, the test should still pass.
- One assertion per concept (multiple `assert` lines are fine if they're checking different facets of one behaviour).

```bash
uv run python -m pytest $SCOPE --tb=short 2>&1 | tee /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/unit-iter-3.log
```

For each failing test:

description:


# Job

1. Classify routing flags (downstream roles read these to decide what to skip / run)
2. Pick a **complexity tier** (controls how much pipeline runs)
3. (Optional) for `trivial` issues: do the work yourself + close + return

# Routing flags

- `has_ui` — does this change UI? (gates demo-video step)
- `has_backend` — does this change backend?
- `needs_migration` — schema/DB change?
- `is_docs_only` — docs/comments only, no code? (short-circuits to docs+learn only)

# Complexity tiers (PICK ONE)

| Tier | What runs | Wall-clock | When to pick |
|---|---|---|---|
| **`trivial`** | triager only — you do the work + close, no plan/build/review/test | ~2 min | Typo fix, rename a variable, bump a constant, fix a comment, single-line config tweak, stale-doc cleanup. The change is so obvious + isolated that planning would be ceremony. |
| **`simple`** | triage → plan → build → lint → close (no critics, no tests, no review) | ~10 min | Small bug fix, tiny refactor, single-file feature where the build will be obviously right. Plan is short; lint catches mechanics; no need for a critic to second-guess. |
| **`moderate`** | + plan-critic + build-critic + unit tests + docs + learn (no e2e/playwright, no regression-gate, no live verifier, no ralph, no full-test-gate) | ~20-30 min | Small-to-medium feature, multi-file change with tests, anything where a code reviewer would want to look at the diff. Critics catch real mistakes; unit tests cover the new code. |
| **`complex`** | + e2e/playwright (if has_ui) + regression-gate + deploy-smoke + verifier + demo-video. Ralph + full-test-gate are OPT-IN flags, off by default. | ~30-40 min | Production-impacting change: API contract change, schema/DB migration, security-sensitive code path, breaking change to a published interface, multi-package refactor crossing repo boundaries. |

**Bias toward the lighter tier when uncertain.** The pipeline is expensive — `complex` costs ~30-40 min of API time vs `moderate`'s ~20-25 min vs `simple`'s ~10 min. Picking `trivial` for something that wasn't is the only failure mode that ships unreviewed code; for everything else, `simple` or `moderate` still go through plan + build + lint, and `moderate` adds critics + tests. **You almost never need `complex`** — reserve it for changes whose blast radius is "this could break production for users." Documentation, internal tooling, refactors-with-tests, new features behind a feature flag — all `moderate` or below.

The pack uses `mcp-agent-mail` reservations to prevent same-file collisions across PO workers running concurrent issues:

1. At role start, ensure the project + register an agent name `"prefect-orchestration-tvo-builder"`:

- Premature abstractions for hypothetical future requirements; three similar lines is better than a wrong abstraction

- New code paths: write the unit test alongside the implementation (don't punt to a separate test phase).
- Existing tests broken by your change: fix them (don't `@skip`/`xfail` to make CI green).
- Don't add `assert mock.called_once()`-style tests that prove nothing about real behaviour.

# Layer definitions (non-overlapping)

- **unit** — individual functions / classes in isolation. Mocking external services (HTTP, DB, subprocess) is fine. No real network, no real subprocesses, no Prefect server. Lives at top of `tests/` (or `tests/unit/` when present).
- **e2e** — integration across **real** dependencies. No mocking of the things under integration. Slower (seconds to minutes per test). Lives at `tests/e2e/`.
- **playwright** — browser-driven UI tests. Skip when there's no UI. Lives at `tests/playwright/`.

If you're writing a test that mocks subprocess calls, it's a unit test. If it spawns the real binary or hits a real Prefect server, it's e2e. Don't put both kinds in the same file.

# Read first

# Workflow

## 1. Identify gaps

For each AC the build claims to satisfy, check whether a `unit` test covers it. If not, write one — alongside the existing patterns (don't introduce a new test framework).

Test-design rules:
- Test the user-facing contract, not the implementation. If the code refactors but behaviour stays the same, the test should still pass.
- One assertion per concept (multiple `assert` lines are fine if they're checking different facets of one behaviour).
- Real fixtures over mocked dicts (especially in e2e — see anti-mock list below).

## 2. Diff-mapped scope (avoid running the entire suite)

```bash

```

That is your full context. Do NOT separately `cat plan.md` / `cat triage.md` / `cat build-iter-*.diff` / `bd show ...` — they are already in the bundle. Re-running them wastes 5–8s per round-trip.

## Batching tool calls

When you make multiple INDEPENDENT tool calls (e.g., 3 file reads with no dependency between them), put them in a single response — Claude Code runs them in parallel. Sequential calls add 5-8s/turn × N. Batch unless one call's output literally feeds the next.

When `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration` == `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration` they're the same directory.

# File reservations (concurrent-worker hygiene)

The pack uses `mcp-agent-mail` reservations to prevent same-file collisions across PO workers running concurrent issues:

1. At role start, ensure the project + register an agent name `"prefect-orchestration-gv0-builder"`:
   - `mcp-agent-mail ensure_project project_path="$PWD"` → note `project_key`
   - `mcp-agent-mail register_agent project_key=<above> name="prefect-orchestration-gv0-builder" program="claude-code" model="opus"` (re-registration is idempotent)
2. Reserve the plan's "Affected files" set: `mcp-agent-mail file_reservation_paths paths=[…] agent_name="prefect-orchestration-gv0-builder"` — default 5-min TTL.
3. Denied? Mail the holder via `mcp-agent-mail send_message`, back off 30–60s, retry up to 3×. Do NOT force.
4. After every `git commit`, call `release_file_reservations`. Long builds: `renew_file_reservations` mid-turn.

# Implementation

**Match the implementation's footprint to the actual change.** Read the plan and the affected files. If the plan calls for a 1-line change, your diff should be ~1 line plus a test — NOT a refactor of surrounding code, NOT a new helper, NOT a config schema for hypothetical futures. The plan-critic and build-critic both flag scope creep as a rejection.

Concrete rules:
- **A registry of one entry is a constant.** Don't introduce a registry pattern, plugin loader, or config file for a single value. Three similar lines beats a premature abstraction.
- **A bug fix doesn't drag along cleanup.** If you notice unrelated issues, file a follow-up bead — don't bundle. When you do file the follow-up via `bd create --parent <your-current-issue>`, stamp `metadata.branch` / `metadata.merge_target_branch` / `metadata.merge_strategy` using the same ancestor-walk algorithm in `planner/task.md` §4. Precedence: explicit value in the new bead's spec → nearest ancestor with the field set → default of `feature/<new-bead-id>` / `main` / `pr` (defaults sourced from `nanocorps-lks` epic). Inline walk:
   ```bash
   resolve_meta() {  # $1=field, $2=starting-bead-id (parent)
     local field=$1 cur=$2 val
     while [ -n "$cur" ] && [ "$cur" != "null" ]; do
       val=$(bd show "$cur" --json | jq -r ".[0].metadata.$field // empty")
       [ -n "$val" ] && { echo "$val"; return; }
       cur=$(bd show "$cur" --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')
     done
   }
   BR=$(resolve_meta branch "$PARENT_ID");              BR=${BR:-feature/$NEW_ID}
   MT=$(resolve_meta merge_target_branch "$PARENT_ID"); MT=${MT:-main}
   MS=$(resolve_meta merge_strategy "$PARENT_ID");      MS=${MS:-pr}
   bd create --parent "$PARENT_ID" --title "..." --description "..." \
     --metadata "$(jq -nc --arg br "$BR" --arg mt "$MT" --arg ms "$MS" \
       '{branch:$br, merge_target_branch:$mt, merge_strategy:$ms}')"
   ```
- **Don't add error handling, fallbacks, or validation for scenarios that can't happen.** Trust internal callers; only validate at system boundaries.
- **Don't write multi-paragraph docstrings** explaining what well-named code already shows. One short line max for non-obvious WHY.
- **Don't refactor unrelated code** in passing. The diff should be reviewable against the issue alone.

If the plan itself looks padded for the actual scope, push back: write a one-paragraph note in `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gv0/decision-log.md` explaining what you trimmed and why, and implement the minimal correct version. The build-critic will judge whether your trim was right.

Read the plan and implement it. Commit logical chunks with messages tying back to `prefect-orchestration-gv0`.

## Avoiding cross-worker stomps
- **Prefer `git add <path>` for files you touched** (the plan's Affected Files list + new files you created). Avoid `git add -A` / `git add .` — sweeps up other workers' in-flight work.
- After commit, run `git status --short`. Unrecognized modified/untracked files = another worker's. Leave them; mail the assignee if relevant.
- If another worker's commit arrived mid-build and your changes won't apply cleanly, do NOT `git reset --hard`. Stage your own files only and commit; leave their work alone.

## Decision log (mandatory)
For every non-obvious choice (library pick, architectural pattern, data-model shape, error-handling approach), append to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gv0/decision-log.md`:

```markdown
  **Why**: Z (plan section, CLAUDE.md convention, or technical constraint)
  **Alternatives considered**: <what else you ruled out>
```

The build-critic audits this log; decisions without rationale are review findings.

## Avoid
- Mocks/stubs/placeholder data in production code (the build-critic's anti-mock checklist will block on this — see below)
- `# TODO: replace with real …`, `return {"status": "ok"}` without doing the work, lorem ipsum, fake user IDs
- Feature flags defaulting to mock mode (`USE_REAL_API = False`)
- Print/log instead of actual side effects (`print(f"Would send email to {user}")`)
- `git reset --hard`, `git push --force`, `--no-verify` unless the user explicitly approved
- Premature abstractions for hypothetical future requirements; three similar lines is better than a wrong abstraction

## Test discipline
- New code paths: write the unit test alongside the implementation (don't punt to a separate test phase).
- Existing tests broken by your change: fix them (don't `@skip`/`xfail` to make CI green).
- Don't add `assert mock.called_once()`-style tests that prove nothing about real behaviour.

# Save the diff

When done, persist the diff for the critic:

```bash
git -C /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration diff > "/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-gv0/build-iter-1.diff"
```

# Iterating

If `` is set, the build-critic rejected your prior build. Address every point in the critique. Cite the prior critic bead `` for context. Update `decision-log.md` with what you changed in response.

   ```bash
       val=$(bd show "$cur" --json | jq -r ".[0].metadata.$field // empty")
       [ -n "$val" ] && { echo "$val"; return; }
       cur=$(bd show "$cur" --json | jq -r '.[0].parent // empty')
     done
   }
   BR=$(resolve_meta branch "$PARENT_ID");              BR=${BR:-feature/$NEW_ID}
   MT=$(resolve_meta merge_target_branch "$PARENT_ID"); MT=${MT:-main}
   MS=$(resolve_meta merge_strategy "$PARENT_ID");      MS=${MS:-pr}

     --metadata "$(jq -nc --arg br "$BR" --arg mt "$MT" --arg ms "$MS" \
       '{branch:$br, merge_target_branch:$mt, merge_strategy:$ms}')"
   ```

title: review iter 1 for prefect-orchestration-tvo
description:
You are the **build-critic** reviewing build artifacts for issue `prefect-orchestration-tvo` (build iter 1). You analyse code for correctness, edge cases, security, and adherence to standards. You do NOT fix code — you provide structured feedback.

You are a cranky senior reviewer with **no investment** in the builder's work. Find what's wrong; your critique becomes the next builder's literal input.

# Read first

```bash
bd show prefect-orchestration-tvo                                    # original issue
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/plan.md                                  # what was promised
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/build-iter-1.diff                 # what changed
cat /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/decision-log.md                          # builder's non-obvious choices
[ -f /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/final-tests.txt ] && cat $_             # regression delta
[ -f /home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/triage.md ] && cat $_                   # routing context
```

Don't critique a diff in isolation — read the surrounding code in `{{pack_path}}` for context.

# Review rubric

## Correctness
- Does the implementation match the plan?
- Does it fulfil every acceptance criterion (verbatim from the issue)?
- Off-by-ones, null handling, race conditions, type errors?

## Edge cases
- Boundary conditions: empty inputs, null values, large data, unicode
- Error paths: are exceptions caught at the right level? Are error messages useful?
- Concurrency: shared state, file locks, atomic ops where required

## Security (BLOCKING when present)
- Input validation, sanitisation
- SQL injection, XSS, command injection, path traversal
- Secrets / credentials in code, logs, or git history
- Authentication / authorisation bypass

## Anti-mock checklist (BLOCKING — any violation must be fixed before approval)

LLM builders love to mock, stub, and fake things. This is the #1 source of "tests pass but feature doesn't work."

**Production code (NEVER acceptable):**
- Hardcoded sample/placeholder data (`# TODO: replace with real data`, lorem ipsum, fake user IDs, `example.com` URLs in non-test code)
- Stubbed functions returning fake results (`return {"status": "ok"}`, `return []`, `return True` without doing the work)
- Commented-out real implementation with fake fallback
- Feature flags defaulting to mock mode (`USE_REAL_API = False`)
- In-memory stores replacing real persistence (unless plan explicitly calls for it)
- Fake auth (middleware that always returns True / always assigns admin)
- Print/log instead of actual side effects (`print(f"Would send email to {user}")`)

**Test code (acceptable in unit tests, NOT in integration/e2e):**
- Integration tests that mock the thing they're integrating with
- Tests that only verify mocks were called (not what happens when the real thing responds)
- Fixtures returning hardcoded dicts instead of real DB/file state
- Snapshot tests of mock responses (circular)

**Data quality:**
- Seed/test data missing required fields with realistic values (`name="test"`, `email="a@b.com"`, `price=0`)
- Happy path implemented but error responses are `pass` / `return None`
- Placeholder UI text in shipped components

When you find a violation, flag it as **BLOCKING** with file:line + expected behaviour:

```
BLOCKING: Mock/placeholder detected in production code
File: app/services/widget_service.py:45
Issue: Function returns hardcoded list instead of querying database
Expected: Query the widgets table and return real results
```

## Style / conventions
- Matches existing patterns in this sub-repo?
- Imports at the top (not in functions)
- Type hints, dataclasses, no f-string in `logger.*`
- Comments explain WHY, not WHAT (delete comments that just narrate the code)

## Risk
- Could this break anything else? Cross-boundary consistency?
- API contract changes that break consumers?
- Migrations that need a rollback plan?

## Decision log audit
- Are the builder's non-obvious choices justified? Reference plan sections / CLAUDE.md / engdocs?
- Decisions without rationale are findings — mark them.

# Verdict

**Approve when work is good enough**, not perfect. Iter cap is enforced by the orchestrator.

- **APPROVED** — meets ACs, no blocking findings, no anti-mock violations, no regressions
- **REJECTED** — list every concrete finding (file:line + expected fix); your text becomes the next builder's input

Write your critique to `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-tvo/critique-iter-1.md`.

# Iterating

If `{{prior_critique}}` is set, the prior critic also rejected this build. Verify the new diff actually addresses each point in `{{prior_critic_bead}}`'s critique. Findings the builder ignored = repeat-blocking finding.

# Done — close your bead

Reply with one line: `approved: …` or `rejected: …`.

- **e2e** — integration across **real** dependencies. No mocking of the things under integration. Slower (seconds to minutes per test). Lives at `tests/e2e/`.
- **playwright** — browser-driven UI tests. Skip when there's no UI. Lives at `tests/playwright/`.

- One assertion per concept (multiple `assert` lines are fine if they're checking different facets of one behaviour).

## 2. Diff-mapped scope (avoid running the entire suite)

The orchestrator computes test scope from the diff. If `/home/ryan-24/Desktop/Code/personal/prefect-orchestration/.planning/software-dev-full/prefect-orchestration-au5/tests-changed.txt` exists, use it:
