title: po serve: pluggable PG creds (drop hardcoded prefect:prefect)
description: Today `po serve install` hardcodes Postgres user/password/db as `prefect:prefect/prefect` on 127.0.0.1:5432, with no way to override. Fine for a single-user dev box; broken anywhere shared (multi-user host, CI runner, future remote-PG topology) and a security smell. Add proper credential plumbing.
acceptance_criteria: (1) `po serve install` accepts `--pg-user`, `--pg-password`, `--pg-db`, `--pg-port`, `--pg-host` flags. (2) Without flags, generate a random password on first install and store creds in a 0600 file (e.g. `~/.config/po/serve.env`) sourced by both systemd units. (3) Re-running `po serve install` reuses the existing creds file (no rotation unless `--rotate-password`). (4) `PREFECT_API_DATABASE_CONNECTION_URL` is set from the same source so the profile stays in sync. (5) `po serve uninstall --purge-data` also removes the creds file. (6) Docs in `CLAUDE.md` updated. (7) Optional: `--external-pg postgresql://...` skips the docker container entirely and just configures Prefect to use a user-supplied PG instance.
notes: Context: shipped `po serve` in 2026-04 to fix SQLite "database is locked" under 17 concurrent flows. Rolled with hardcoded creds for v1 since target was a single workstation. See conversation circa 2026-04-26 + `prefect_orchestration/serve.py` for the unit templates that need parametrization.

title: email-triage example pack — emit_event poller + automation-driven triager
description:

title: po-stripe reference pack — first tool pack per engdocs/pack-convention.md
description: First reference implementation of the tool-pack convention from engdocs/pack-convention.md. po-stripe is a Python package that depends on the stripe SDK, ships a SKILL.md teaching agents how to use Stripe safely in this nanocorp's context, and registers a couple of po.commands + po.doctor_checks. No PO-level typed client, no wrappers — agents call the stripe SDK directly via the skill-provided examples.
design:
## Repo layout

  po-stripe/
  ├── pyproject.toml            stripe>=9.0 (SDK fallback); po.commands + po.doctor_checks
  ├── po_stripe/
  │   ├── __init__.py
  │   ├── commands.py           thin wrappers over stripe CLI
  │   └── checks.py             doctor checks
  ├── skills/
  │   └── stripe/
  │       └── SKILL.md          CLI-first, links vendor llms.txt, nanocorp rules
  ├── overlay/
  │   └── CLAUDE.md             reinforcement
  └── README.md

## Tool-access order (per engdocs/pack-convention.md)

  1. stripe CLI (primary) — 'stripe charges create --amount 2000 --currency usd ...'
  2. stripe Python SDK (fallback for webhooks/streaming)
  3. HTTPS API (unused in v1)
  4. MCP (unused)

SKILL.md leads with CLI recipes; SDK section is marked 'fallback'. Links to:
  - https://docs.stripe.com/stripe-cli
  - https://docs.stripe.com/api
  - https://docs.stripe.com/llms.txt    (vendor's AI-friendly docs)
  - https://docs.stripe.com/projects    (scoped API keys)

## Entry points

  [project.entry-points."po.commands"]
  stripe-balance = "po_stripe.commands:balance"
  stripe-recent  = "po_stripe.commands:recent_charges"
  stripe-mode    = "po_stripe.commands:mode"

  [project.entry-points."po.doctor_checks"]
  stripe-cli-installed = "po_stripe.checks:cli_installed"
  stripe-env           = "po_stripe.checks:env_set"
  stripe-api           = "po_stripe.checks:api_reachable"

## Commands (CLI-native, thin shells)

  po stripe-balance    → shells out to 'stripe balance retrieve'
  po stripe-recent     → 'stripe charges list --limit 10' then tabulates
  po stripe-mode       → inspects STRIPE_API_KEY prefix, returns test/live + project

## Doctor checks

  stripe-cli-installed: shutil.which('stripe'); red with hint 'brew install stripe/stripe-cli/stripe' if missing
  stripe-env: STRIPE_API_KEY set? valid prefix? (sk_test_ for dev / sk_live_ for prod)
  stripe-api: 'stripe balance retrieve' exits 0 and returns within 5s

## Nanocorp-specific rules in SKILL.md

  - Always use test keys (sk_test_) in dev; doctor check warns on live keys in dev envs
  - Idempotency key convention: --idempotency-key "{issue_id}:{step_name}"
  - Charges > $500 require 'bd human --question=...' approval before creation
  - Refund flow: prefer 'stripe refunds create --charge <id>' over PaymentIntent reuse
notes: Depends on 4ja.4 (overlay + skills merging) and 4ja.1 (po install/packs CLI). Blocks on parity epic completing those.
close_reason: Completed: po-stripe reference tool pack (3 commands, 3 doctor checks, CLI-first SKILL.md, overlay CLAUDE.md). Confidence: HIGH. Verified live: po packs install + list, po doctor surfaces stripe-cli-installed/stripe-env/stripe-api, po stripe-mode works, 28/28 unit tests pass. Criteria 1-7,9 met; (8) depends on 4ja.4 session-start copy already shipped.

title: po.doctor_checks entry-point group — packs extend po doctor
description: 'po doctor' today runs core wiring checks only. Packs should be able to ship their own checks (integration auth works, pack-specific data dirs exist, external deps reachable, etc.) and have 'po doctor' aggregate them.
design:
New entry-point group 'po.doctor_checks'. Each entry resolves to a callable returning a DoctorCheck(name, status: Literal['green','yellow','red'], message, hint).

  [project.entry-points."po.doctor_checks"]
  software-dev-rig = "po_formulas.checks:rig_has_planning_dir"
  claude-auth      = "po_formulas.checks:claude_cli_authenticated"

'po doctor' runs core checks first (bd, prefect server, pools, pack integrity), then each pack's checks in install order. Output is one unified table; critical-red gives exit 1. Each check has a soft timeout (default 5s) so a slow check can't hang the whole run.
acceptance_criteria: (1) po.doctor_checks entry-point group defined; (2) 'po doctor' runs core + all pack checks in one table; (3) checks have name/status/message/hint; (4) 5s per-check timeout, yellow on timeout; (5) po-formulas-software-dev ships at least one example check; (6) documented in CLAUDE.md.
notes: Principles §1 + §3 (pack-contributed surface). Depends on 08y for 'po install' to register checks. (superseded prefect-orchestration-99u)
close_reason: po-25aeb3b8 complete

title: overlay/ dir copied into agent cwd at session start
description:
Packs ship two kinds of content files that need to reach the agent's working directory at session start:

1. overlay/** — arbitrary files (CLAUDE.md snippets, scripts/, .env.example, etc.). Copied into rig cwd.
2. skills/<name>/SKILL.md — Claude Code skills. Copied into <rig>/.claude/skills/<pack-name>/<name>/ so Claude auto-picks them up. See engdocs/pack-convention.md.
design:
At session creation, AgentSession (or the flow that constructs it) walks every installed pack (discovered via importlib.metadata) and for each pack:

  (a) if pack contains overlay/** — merge into cwd, skip files already tracked by git or already existing unless pack overlay wins via an explicit flag.
  (b) if pack contains skills/<name>/SKILL.md — copy into <rig>/.claude/skills/<pack-name>/<name>/SKILL.md. Rig-scoped (not global) so different rigs can have different skill sets. Claude Code auto-discovers from .claude/skills.

Use shutil.copytree with a skip-existing predicate for (a). For (b), always overwrite — skills are pack-owned canonical content.

Optional per-role overlay: <pack>/po_<module>/agents/<role>/overlay/** stacks on top of pack-wide overlay.

Cleanup: on session end, remove overlay-origin files not modified by the agent (optional v2). v1: copy + leave, user gitignores what they don't want committed.
acceptance_criteria: (1) <pack>/overlay/** copied into session cwd at start; (2) <pack>/skills/<name>/SKILL.md copied into <rig>/.claude/skills/<pack-name>/<name>/SKILL.md; (3) existing files in cwd not overwritten by overlay (skip-existing); (4) skills always overwritten from pack (canonical); (5) per-role overlay stacks on pack-wide; (6) opt-out via AgentSession(overlay=False, skills=False); (7) documented in pack-convention.md; (8) tested with po-stripe reference pack (skills/stripe/SKILL.md delivers to <rig>/.claude/skills/po-stripe/stripe/SKILL.md).
notes: Principles §1 + §3 (pack-contributed surface). Small. Natural companion to 2rt (agents/ folder layout). (superseded prefect-orchestration-0gf)
close_reason: po-ffc1581c complete

title: overlay/ dir copied into agent cwd at session start
description: Packs often need a standard set of files present in the agent's working directory every turn (a CLAUDE.md snippet, a scratch dir, a .prompts/ dir, etc.) without committing them to the rig's real git repo. Support a 'overlay/' dir in the pack that AgentSession copies into cwd at session start.
design:
Pack convention: <pack>/overlay/<arbitrary-structure>. At session creation, AgentSession (or the flow that constructs it) copies the overlay tree into session cwd, overwriting nothing already tracked by git there. Use rsync-style update semantics (or plain shutil.copytree with a skip-existing predicate).

Optional: per-role overlay directories <pack>/agents/<role>/overlay/ that stack on top of the pack-wide one.

Cleanup: on session end (or ClaudeCliBackend.run exit), remove any files that were overlay-origin and not modified by the agent. Or punt — leave them, rely on git ignore. Simplest first version: copy + leave, user's problem.
acceptance_criteria: (1) Pack <pack>/overlay/ tree is copied into cwd at session start; (2) existing files in cwd not overwritten; (3) per-role overlay stacks on top of pack-wide; (4) opt-out via AgentSession(overlay=False); (5) documented in CLAUDE.md.
notes: Principles §1 + §3 (pack-contributed surface). Small. Natural companion to 2rt (agents/ folder layout).
close_reason: superseded by prefect-orchestration-4ja.4

title: po run graph <root-id> — fan out any bead sub-graph as a Prefect DAG
description: Generalize 'po run epic' into 'po run graph'. Takes any bead ID as root and fans out every reachable descendant (via bd-dep edges: parent-child, blocks, tracks) as a Prefect DAG with wait_for= wired from the graph. No naming convention required (no dot-suffix assumption), no 'epic' status required. Works for: an epic (today's case), a feature bead with sub-tasks, an ad-hoc tag-rooted subset, a convoy bead, a temporary grouping-bead a human just made to batch 5 things. 'epic_run' becomes a thin wrapper: 'po run epic X' == 'po run graph X --traverse=parent-child,blocks'.
design:
## Shape

po run graph <root-id> \n  --rig <name> \n  --rig-path <path> \n  [--traverse=parent-child,blocks,tracks]   # which edge types to follow (default: parent-child,blocks)
  [--formula=software-dev-full]              # what to run per node (default: software-dev-full)
  [--max-issues=N]                           # test-gate
  [--include-closed]                         # default: skip closed; useful for re-running
  [--root-as-node]                           # default: root is a container, not run; set this if root itself is work

## Algorithm

1. BFS from <root-id> via 'bd list --json' and follow the allowed edge types.
2. Filter out closed (unless --include-closed) and the root itself (unless --root-as-node).
3. Build a sub-DAG whose edges are the 'blocks' dependencies between collected nodes.
4. Topo-sort; refuse on cycle.
5. For each node in order, submit the chosen formula with wait_for= set to futures of its blocker deps (same as today's epic_run).

## Why this is the right shape

- 'epic' is a naming accident. The real primitive is 'a set of beads with a dep graph'. Every grouping concept (epic, convoy, tag-selection, ad-hoc container) collapses to 'what's the root?' + 'which edges count?'.
- Makes 'bd dep' the first-class way to express batch intent, not ID naming.
- Enables 'po run graph <tag>' once bd-tag queries land — 'run formula X on everything tagged 'release-blocker' in dep order'.
- Makes 'po run graph' a good home for future --since/--assignee/--priority filters on the node set.

## Backward compat

- 'po run epic' keeps working. Internally becomes a thin wrapper calling graph traversal with parent-child+blocks.
- Tests for the existing epic path continue to pass unchanged.
notes: Principles: §1 (nothing Prefect can do here — graph source is bd) + §2 (CLI-first).\nRelated: prefect-orchestration-h5s (discover-via-deps for epic_run) — this is the generalization; h5s can be absorbed / closed-as-superseded if this one lands first, or ship h5s as a stepping stone.\nDepends on nothing; blocks nothing (h5s is a lighter-weight stepping stone).
close_reason: po-9aec14bc complete

title: po run --time <when> — future-scheduled one-shot runs
description: Currently 'po run <formula> --args' fires synchronously in-process. For 'run this one issue in 2h / at 09:00 tomorrow' there is no PO-native path — users have to register a manual deployment and call 'prefect deployment run --start-in 2h'. Extend 'po run' with an optional '--time <when>' that resolves the pack's manual deployment by convention and submits a one-shot scheduled run to the connected Prefect server.
design:
## Shape

    po run software-dev-full --time 2h --issue-id sr-8yu.6 --rig site --rig-path /path/to/site
    po run software-dev-full --time '2026-04-25T09:00:00-04:00' --issue-id ...
    po run software-dev-full --time '+30m' ...

If '--time' is omitted → current synchronous behavior (unchanged).

## --time parsing

Accept:
  * relative: '2h', '30m', '1d', '+30m' → timedelta, passed to Prefect as start_in
  * absolute ISO-8601 with tz → parsed to datetime, passed as start_at
Implementation: pendulum or stdlib (datetime + a tiny regex for the relative form). No new required dep.

## Deployment-name convention

For each formula 'X' registered via 'po.formulas' entry points, the pack should also register a manual deployment named '<formula>-manual' via 'po.deployments' — unscheduled, parameters default-filled. 'po run --time' looks up '<formula>-manual' from the Prefect server (prefect_client) and calls '.run(…)' with the parsed start_in/start_at.

Fallback when '<formula>-manual' is not registered: fail with a helpful message: 'formula software-dev-full has no manual deployment; run po deploy --apply first, or add software_dev_full.to_deployment(name="software-dev-full-manual") to your pack.deployments.register().'

## First-principle consistency

Passes the test in engdocs/principles.md §1 (Thin CLI over Prefect): resolves the pack's manual deployment name by convention so users don't grep po deploy output. Collapses 'prefect deployment run <pack>/<formula>-manual --start-in 2h --param …' into 'po run <formula> --time 2h --…'. Prefect alone can't do that lookup.
acceptance_criteria:
(1) 'po run <formula> --time 2h' parses '2h' as a relative duration and submits a scheduled run for deployment '<formula>-manual' via prefect_client, starting in 2h.
(2) 'po run <formula> --time <ISO-8601>' submits with start_at set to the parsed datetime (tz required).
(3) '--time' omitted → existing synchronous behavior unchanged.
(4) Missing deployment → clear error message pointing at the fix (register <formula>-manual and po deploy --apply).
(5) No new required deps; pendulum-or-stdlib implementation.
(6) Worker requirement surfaced in CLI output: 'queued for <time>; ensure prefect worker start --pool po is running before then.'
(7) engdocs/principles.md §1 cited in the design doc / PR body.
notes: Related: prefect-orchestration-shj (po deploy), prefect-orchestration-pw4 (rig-path vs pack-path). This issue depends on shj landing first; pw4 is orthogonal.
close_reason: po-9e803de0 complete

title: po --at: accept space-separated date format (not just strict ISO-8601)
description:
\`po run --at\` / \`po resume --at\` / \`po retry --at\` currently reject space-separated datetime formats with timezone, requiring strict ISO-8601 with \`T\` separator.

## Repro

\`\`\`
$ po resume prefect-orchestration-tvo --rig prefect-orchestration --at "2026-04-30 19:00 EDT"
error: failed to schedule resume for 'software-dev-full': bad --at '2026-04-30 19:00 EDT': expected relative (2h, 30m, 1d, +30m) or ISO-8601 with timezone

$ po resume prefect-orchestration-tvo --rig prefect-orchestration --at "2026-04-30T19:00:00-04:00"
scheduled flow-run 1d8b8d61-... at 2026-04-30T23:00:00+00:00
\`\`\`

## Why

The space-separated form (\`YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM TZ\`) is what \`date\`, most CLIs, and operators type by hand. The error message even hints both ISO-8601 forms should work. Strict-only ISO-8601 with \`T\` separator and offset like \`-04:00\` is friction for ad-hoc scheduling.

## Spec

Accept these forms in addition to current ones:

- \`2026-04-30 19:00\` — local timezone assumed
- \`2026-04-30 19:00 EDT\` — named timezone
- \`2026-04-30 19:00 -04:00\` — offset with space
- \`19:00\` — today, local timezone
- \`19:00 EDT\` — today, named timezone
- \`tomorrow 09:00\` — natural-language relative (stretch)

Use \`dateutil.parser.parse\` (loose) as the fallback after the strict ISO-8601 + relative-duration parsers. Reject only if all parsers fail.

## Acceptance criteria

- All four space-separated forms above parse successfully and schedule the flow at the right UTC time
- Existing \`--at 2h\` / \`--at +30m\` / \`--at 2026-04-30T19:00:00-04:00\` formats still work
- Error message lists at least one space-separated example alongside ISO-8601

## Related

- Implemented in same PR as prefect-orchestration-pm3 (now closed): \`--at\` on \`po resume\` / \`po retry\`.
acceptance_criteria: space-separated 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM TZ' formats parse correctly; existing relative + strict-ISO-8601 still work; error message updated with space-separated example

title: [mail:po-smoketest] fix X
description:
please address X

---
From: critic
close_reason: read

value: PO formulas should be bead graphs, not flow-body loops. Eureka rediscovery of the gas-city formula model. Core idea: each subtask = its own bead; agent's last action is bd close (or bd update with notes); critic emits verdicts by *creating a new bead* with the prior verdict copied into its description (NOT by mutating the parent — keeps each bead self-contained so the next agent only ever has to read one bead). Iteration loops = critic opens <parent>.build.iter<N+1> with --blocks; PO watches dolt change events on the dep graph and dispatches. Kills tmux turn-end-detection problem entirely (bd close IS the signal), drops verdicts/<step>.json + prompt_for_verdict + most of software_dev.py's loop scaffolding (~600 LOC -> ~50). Open design point: role-session affinity — a 'builder' Claude session should be able to claim multiple successive build beads with its prior --resume <uuid> intact (so it keeps context across iter1->iter2), not fork fresh per bead. Means bead claim needs to map back to a long-lived per-role session, probably via metadata.json keyed by role rather than by bead. Tracking epic to be created.

Schemas are for the consumer, not the producer. When a flow's pause/resume input is consumed by deterministic code, use a typed Pydantic RunInput. When it's consumed by another LLM call (which can read free-form intent), use wait_for_input=str — the LLM is the schema. Don't pay the rigidity tax for type-safety the consumer doesn't need. Same principle as Claude Code prompting 'A/B/C?' and accepting 'C but skip step 3' — the interpreter has comprehension. For nanocorp pause_flow_run usage: default to wait_for_input=str + LLM-interpreter on resume; reserve typed RunInput for the rare case where downstream is dumb code that needs the constraint, or where you want the UI form to guide a human (dropdown of known options, structured data entry).
